sentences.text: The one I said that we going to perish?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Emigration.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So, these boys, the two Americans, he went-- he-- they went back.
And they told my family there-- whoever-- they told that we are survived, be it, the two of us.
So they send us affidavits.
And on the base of affidavits and the ground of affidavits, I could get a Polish passport.
And then-- but I couldn't come here, because Stalin wouldn't let me come here-- only it opened up the-- Israel, and all I could leave for to go to Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
When the fight was going on.
The fight was going on.
It wasn't yet Israel.
It was sort of.
They had a-- we were going with the organization.
I think I forgot to tell you that when I was yet in Warsaw, there-- there was a Jewish in the military-- a Jewish colonel who-- a communist, which I wasn't in my lifetime.
I didn't belong in my life to any party in the world.
So news came to the-- my sister had a big mouth.
And she was telling them what's going on-- what we trying to do-- that we have relatives.
We're going to leave.
And the Poles didn't want to know that part.
So one Sunday morning, we are still in bed.
Somebody's at the door, and gave me a invitation, a call, to go to another part of Warsaw, far away, and to be investigated or something-- whatever you want to call it.
And my sister knew that she the trouble maker-- that she caused me all this.
If she wouldn't talk, we wouldn't have all this.
She got scared.
I go with you.
I say, you don't have-- I want to go with you.
She felt guilty what she did to me-- didn't have to talk.
Here, take with you cookies.
Take with you a napkin.
And-- and we went there.
And we came in front to a door where you had to wait.
In the door--
They had us wait.
And the door had cushions.
So you were waiting outside.
You can't hear what they talking inside.
Then they called me in.
And they-- he said, is that true that you want to leave Poland?
I said, yes.
What the reason?
I said, no specific reason.
I have relatives.
Did anybody of the government abuse you, or offend you, or harm you in any way?
No.
I have nothing against the government-- all kinds of questions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, if that case, you free to go.
That's how it was.
She went two years before me.
I didn't want to go at all.
She went two years before me.
I was going to Marseilles.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They took us, the-- who was married that time.
I didn't.
I wasn't.
They took us into a train going to Marseilles.
And them husbands, whoever had a-- they were not allowed to go underground because of politics.
So they were flying.
And the wives and the husband met in Marseilles.
In Marseilles, we stay-- stayed about 10 days.
And one Saturday night, it came about secretly from the Arabs-- because the British didn't want to let the Arabs get mad.
So Saturday night came a little boat and took us to Italy, to-- I forgot the name.
It's on the North of Italy.
I forgot the name.
And we were there.
Then we went to Paris.
We stayed in Paris some days.
And from Paris, we went to-- now we leaving Israel or going to Israel?
Going to Israel.
Going to Israel.
That Saturday night, we went to-- in Italy.
I forgot the name.
And we went to Israel.
Military boat, where we were sitting on the floor.
They had soldiers.
They used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's dry bread.
What do you call dry bread in English?
No special name?
It was provision for the soldiers.
And dirty as it was, they had a-- a tub with tea in it-- and dirty as could be.
And that's what we had.
I think it was four days.
But I was very sick-- seasick.
And I was lying there.
And I knew a little Latin.
And the Italian have their own language.

So when I was so sick and my head is hanging down, out the cart like this, because I'm so seasick-- and they felt sorry for me, these cooks or somebody-- the Italians-- and they brought the pea soup for me-- something to eat.
I couldn't eat.
But there was a cup of it, a little baby.
And he looked at me-- or she-- I think the baby was.
Now the boys, the Jewish boys-- I want to stick with you.
So you got over being sick, and you got to Israel?
Yes.
In Israel, we came to the port, and they loaded us in buses.
And they took us to the desert.
I don't know name.
I don't know.
Do you know Pardes Hanna?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us to a desert, and they spilled the load in the desert.
And I was with a girl by name, Anna.
In that case, she was-- she had a uncle to come and pick her up.
But the rest of us had nobody to pick us up.
And, what do you call those animals-- they cry at night?
Coyotes?
They were crying, and I was so scared.
And there was-- the British had these aluminum-covered shacks or something, where they stayed.
I don't know the name.
And when those cried-- and I was very scared.
So Anne went to snoop out.
And there was a-- a woman there, and she let us in.
Because I was afraid to be outdoors at night.
And we stayed there.
And Anne said, I don't care.
My-- my uncle will come and take me home.
In those years in Israel, you couldn't be on the road.
Because they'll come and pick you up as spies.
I-- I-- in Pardes Hanna, there is a place that I had my mother's cousin.
And they had-- how do you say in English [NON-ENGLISH]?
They had those oranges and grapefruits-- those things.
And we-- now-- we met in Marseilles a boy who told me-- I didn't have money.
And he lent me $5 that time.
And I said to-- as soon as I come to Israel, I borrow money from my relatives there, and I'll give it back to you.
And my sister went a week before me there.
She went, and she got to find nurses that we knew from the front.
They live now in-- forgot the name.
And she knew where to go-- not to go to them, but to find employment.
And she left in next week.
Next Saturday night, I came there.
And in the desert, there was this boy.
And I-- he said, I know where your sister is.
I'll get you there.
And he took me there to those relatives.
And she was there.
But there is no point of being there.
We didn't come to sit there.
We came to look for the nurses that we know from before and look for employment.
In meantime I was in a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
--what do you call it?
Where all the-- well, I'll call them refugees.
Was I a refugee or what?
Call it something like this.
And there was this man-- nasty as could be.
And I told him, now-- you see they get the provision when you weren't there.
And I said to him, now, you keep my card.
I am going just to find out where I can be, and I'll come back.
When-- and he betrayed me.
And I come back, and I had trouble.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, I forgot.
When I came to Israel, they had my things kept in the port.
They wouldn't give it to me.
I brought in friends.
They used to say, in Israel, they don't have gas.
It's only electric.
So I bought in-- in-- in fact, a boy that I knew, he lend me some money, and I bought those electric plates.
Yes.
I had my Polish papers that I am a nurse.
And first, they put me into a city clinic.
And I worked there a 1/2 year.
In Tel Aviv, I think.
I think.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Then, I went to look for this friend from the military time.
And I worked in Haifa.
Besides, in Haifa, I had a friend whom-- and she just died-- who we knew from sixth grade, in-- in my hometown.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --wait a minute.
I had left my-- my husband in Poland.
And he was operated for the kidneys or something.
And I asked a Polish friend, come and visit him-- if he needs something.
He was right there operated when I had to leave.
Then he came to Israel.
And then we had to marry again.
And I worked in Jaffa-- in Jaffa-- in a clinic-- not far from where-- when you come to Israel, where do you live?
So my husband had a-- in the-- second husband-- he had a-- a friend from Galicia.
I don't know.
Those years you had to pay a-- you see, when they came from the front, those people, from the Jewish military, they used to give them places.
So my husband paid him for that room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
When I came to Israel, in 1948, I think the-- was the rest of the fights, like the end of it.
Because the Arabs said, they going to put all of them into the sea!
But they had, in the desert, training people for the [NON-ENGLISH].
He told you before-- in another group-- I forgot now the name.
So these gave the British a big beating, and they ran off.
Close to eight years.
[LAUGHS] I decided to leave before I even went there.
That was the only place I could go, according to Stalin.
[NON-ENGLISH] So then I stayed there extra time, waiting for the visa-- and then 19-- I think 1954, when I finally arrived in New York port-- I think.
Nobody.
My sister was already here.
She came to the port to-- to pick me up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yes, I started to work in a infant's home of Brooklyn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
How did I get there?
There was a-- in New York, an institution called Self-- Self Help for Immigrants of Central Europe.
And I went there.
And they said to me, there is a mother who has four children.
But she is sick, and she has to stay there.
She cannot get home.
But when she will get home, you will have to help her with the newborn baby-- with that baby.
That's how I went there.
When I came there, she was still in the hospital.
And the husband had hands fulls to-- to him-- how to handle those children.
One was 10-years-old-- a bad girl.
The other one was 7.
The other one was 5.
The other one was 2 and 1/2 And I had to start to train them.
And the father said, so what?
When she makes in her pants, so take it and put it on the balcony.
I said that's you.
Or are you handle them, or you go to work, and I'll handle them.
So, I said to her-- I forgot her name.
Now-- he said, when she doesn't want to go to school, just let her.
I say, that's you.
When I'm here, I'll do.
How long did you stay with this family?
I don't even remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Here?
Yes.
Yes.
I was going, as I told you, I had these cassettes from Queen of Angels, from Howard Presbyterian Hospital.
I still have the cassettes.
My-- I had a friend.
I don't know if she's alive or not.
And her, she had, in Los Angeles, cousins of her killed husband.
And she was remarried.
And-- and they said, if your husband-- if you want to come here, we'll find some employment for him.
He wound up to clean carpets.
And-- and she-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --was going to school in the evening with me.
My sister lived across from the school.
So I would leave the infants home, and take my bundles, and stay in my sister's overnight.
And next morning, I go to school-- or the other way around-- something like this.
That's how I learned English.
And when I came to-- to English class, there in New York, the teacher said, English is a crazy language, but it's up to you to know it.
She was before me two years.
Yeah.
No.
On the East coast, yes, but here, nobody.
There is some relative somewheres in Los Angeles on my father's side, but I even forgot his name.
I don't know.
Yes.
I worked until 40 years.
And then in 19-- no-- 1937 to 1977.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I went to Social Security for whatever you call it-- benefits or something?
And the woman said, if you would stay a little longer, you lose out a lot of your benefits.
And I said, I cannot go on another day.
I'd rather take it now or die later.
So that's how it happened.
Now the Germans give me a pension for what they did to us.
So I have Social Security-- that restitution money from the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I only cannot say if-- what to do with.
I can wish for them.
What to do?
I don't know.
They're in a situation they wouldn't have to go through any of this at all.
They're in what you call, in a free land.
Even Israel is not so free.
So what can I tell them?
I would tell them, improve your mind and physical as much as you can.
Study until your mind can't allow you to study-- until you can't.
That I can tell them.
Thank you.
[LAUGHS] Your welcome, 'cause I went through mills until I got where I am now.
